Name: ACM Subjects
LongName: ACM Policy for Research Involving Human Participants and Subjects
Description: Checklist for verifying ethical compliance in computing research involving human participants, including ethical review, consent, participant protection, privacy, and disclosure.
Aim: To help editors and reviewers verify that research involving human participants or identifiable participant data meets ACM's expectations for ethical oversight, informed consent, privacy protection, and transparent disclosure.

Scope.Coverage: Computing and HCI manuscripts that involve living human participants or identifiable participant data, including user studies, surveys, interviews, observational fieldwork, and experiments.
Scope.Focus: Ethical oversight, consent procedures, participant welfare, privacy and data handling, protections for vulnerable groups, and transparency about data sharing and conflicts of interest.
Scope.ContrastWith: This is an ethics-compliance guide rather than a study-design reporting guideline. It complements technical reporting guides by adding a human-subjects ethics layer for computing research.

Aliases: ACM research involving human participants and subjects
EvidenceExamplesPositive: We conducted a user study with 24 participants after institutional review board approval and informed consent.
This HCI experiment involved human participants and includes an ethics statement and consent procedures.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: The manuscript is purely computational and does not involve any human participants or participant data.
This paper reports a systems benchmark with no user study, survey, interview, or participant-facing experiment.
This clinical manuscript reports patients and informed consent, but has no computing, software, HCI, interface, or user-study component.
Matching.Explain: Adds a supporting human-subjects ethics guide for computing manuscripts that involve participant studies or identifiable participant data.

Name: AGREE Reporting Checklist
LongName: AGREE Reporting Checklist: A Tool to Improve Reporting of Clinical Practice Guidelines
Description: The AGREE Reporting Checklist provides guidance on the minimum information that should be reported in clinical practice guidelines to enhance transparency, completeness, and usability. It aligns with the AGREE II instrument domains and supports clear documentation of guideline development processes and recommendations.
Aim: To improve the reporting quality, transparency, and completeness of clinical practice guidelines by specifying essential items that describe their scope, stakeholder involvement, development rigor, clarity, applicability, and editorial independence.

Scope.Coverage: Reports of clinical practice guidelines addressing diagnosis, management, prevention, or treatment in healthcare.
Scope.Focus: Transparent reporting of guideline objectives, development methodology, evidence appraisal, recommendation formulation, implementation considerations, and independence from funding bias.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not for primary research studies, systematic reviews without recommendations, trial protocols, or health technology assessments unless structured as clinical practice guidelines.

Aliases: Appraisal of Guidelines for Research and Evaluation Reporting Checklist
Matching.Explain: Applies to reports of clinical practice guidelines referencing AGREE or structured guideline development and recommendation reporting.

Name: AHA Statistics
LongName: Recommendations for Statistical Reporting in Cardiovascular Medicine
Description: A broad statistical-reporting overlay from the American Heart Association for manuscripts submitted to cardiovascular journals. The document emphasizes cross-cutting statistical reporting standards, points authors to existing design-specific reporting guidelines, and adds AHA-focused expectations for visualization, time-to-event analysis, Bayesian methods, missing data, correlated data, confounding adjustment, and sample-size reporting.
Aim: Improve the clarity, reproducibility, and interpretability of statistical analyses in cardiovascular medicine manuscripts by ensuring that methods and results are reported with enough detail for qualified statistical review and informed clinical reading.

Scope.Coverage: Cardiovascular research manuscripts that report statistical analyses and need a high-level but concrete overlay for methods transparency, interpretation, and design-specific reporting expectations in AHA journals.
Scope.Focus: Detailed description of study population and statistical methods, clinically interpretable scaling and presentation of results, honest visualization, design-specific reporting crosswalks, time-to-event analyses, Bayesian analyses, missing data, correlated data, confounding adjustment, and power and sample-size reporting.
Scope.ContrastWith: This AHA statement is an overlay and curated compendium rather than a replacement for primary design-specific reporting standards such as CONSORT, PRISMA, STARD, TRIPOD, or STROBE. It highlights what cardiovascular statistical reviewers especially need to see in addition to those standards.

Aliases: AHA statistical reporting in cardiovascular medicine
Recommendations for Statistical Reporting in Cardiovascular Medicine
EvidenceExamplesPositive: A cardiovascular outcomes manuscript reports complex statistical analyses including survival models, propensity scores, missing-data handling, and detailed figures, and needs a journal-oriented overlay for statistical transparency.

A Circulation-style manuscript uses a design-specific guideline such as CONSORT or PRISMA but also needs cardiovascular-specific expectations for exact P values, interpretable scaling, Kaplan-Meier presentation, and confounding-adjustment reporting.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A purely mechanistic laboratory paper with no substantive statistical analysis beyond a few descriptive summaries is not a strong match for this AHA overlay.
A non-cardiovascular manuscript that only needs a single design-specific reporting standard and has no special cardiovascular-statistical reporting issues is unlikely to need this overlay.
Matching.Explain: Use this AHA overlay for cardiovascular manuscripts with substantial statistical analysis, especially when reviewers need explicit reporting on methods detail, visualization, survival analysis, confounding adjustment, missing data, or Bayesian analysis in addition to the underlying design-specific guideline.

Name: APA Numbers and Statistics Guide
LongName: APA Style Numbers and Statistics Guide
Description: A concise APA Style instructional guide covering how to write numbers, decimals, and statistical expressions in manuscripts. It provides practical rules for when to use numerals versus words, how to format decimals and p values, and how to present statistical notation consistently in text, tables, and figures.
Aim: Improve consistency, readability, and correctness in APA-style presentation of numbers and statistics across manuscripts, tables, and figures.

Scope.Coverage: APA-style manuscripts that need practical guidance on how to format numbers, decimal places, p values, and statistical notation in prose, tables, and figures.
Scope.Focus: Use of numerals versus words, commas in numbers, decimals and leading zeros, decimal precision for common statistics, exact p-value reporting, mathematical operators, italics for statistical symbols, and abbreviation practices.
Scope.ContrastWith: This guide is a style and formatting overlay, not a methodological or study-design reporting standard. It complements statistical-reporting guidance by telling authors how to present numbers and statistics correctly in APA style.

Aliases: APA Style numbers and statistics guide
EvidenceExamplesPositive: A psychology or social-science manuscript needs guidance on whether to write numbers as numerals or words, how many decimals to report, and how to format p values and statistical symbols in APA style.
An author is preparing APA-style tables and text and needs a formatting overlay for statistical notation rather than a methodological reporting standard.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A manuscript asking for design-specific reporting requirements, such as trial registration or risk-of-bias domains, is not primarily a fit for this APA style guide.
A purely qualitative manuscript with no quantitative results or numerical formatting issues is unlikely to need this overlay.
Matching.Explain: Use this APA style overlay when manuscripts need guidance on how to present numbers, decimals, p values, and statistical notation consistently in APA format.

Name: ARRIVE
LongName: Reporting animal research: Explanation and elaboration for the ARRIVE guidelines 2.0
Description: This Explanation and Elaboration document accompanies ARRIVE 2.0 and provides the rationale, supporting evidence, and practical guidance for reporting each item and sub-item in the ARRIVE 2.0 checklist for animal research. It also includes best-practice advice for the design and conduct of animal studies to improve transparency and reproducibility.
Aim: To support authors, reviewers, and editors in applying ARRIVE 2.0 by explaining the importance of each checklist item and sub-item, clarifying key concepts, and providing guidance and examples to improve the rigour, transparency, and reproducibility of animal research reporting.

Scope.Coverage: In vivo bioscience research involving living animals, including hypothesis-testing and exploratory studies, where transparent reporting of design, conduct, analysis, and welfare considerations is needed for reproducibility and appraisal.
Scope.Focus: Item-by-item explanation and elaboration of ARRIVE 2.0, including rationale and practical guidance for reporting study design (including experimental unit), sample size, inclusion/exclusion and n values, bias mitigation (randomisation/blinding), outcome definitions, statistical methods, animal characteristics and provenance, replicable procedures, complete results reporting, ethical oversight, housing/husbandry, welfare monitoring, interpretation/limitations, generalisability, protocol preparation/registration, data access, and declarations of interests/funding.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not for studies in humans (e.g., CONSORT/STROBE) or evidence syntheses (PRISMA). ARRIVE targets in vivo animal experiments rather than in vitro-only or purely computational studies.

Aliases: Reporting animal research: Explanation and elaboration for the ARRIVE guidelines 2.0
Animal Research: Reporting of In Vivo Experiments
EvidenceExamplesPositive: This in vivo mouse study is reported in accordance with the ARRIVE 2.0 guidelines.
We followed ARRIVE 2.0 (Explanation and Elaboration) to report randomisation, blinding, and humane endpoints.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: We conducted a randomized controlled trial in humans following CONSORT.
This systematic review and meta-analysis was reported according to PRISMA.
Matching.Explain: Routes in vivo animal experimental studies to ARRIVE 2.0 and its accompanying Explanation and Elaboration guidance, excluding human clinical trials, observational epidemiology, protocols, and evidence syntheses.

Name: ASA Statement on p-Values
LongName: The ASA Statement on p-Values: Context, Process, and Purpose
Description: The ASA Statement on p-Values is a cross-disciplinary statistical-inference guideline that clarifies how p-values should and should not be interpreted. It emphasizes that p-values are limited summaries of incompatibility between data and model assumptions, warns against threshold-based decision making and selective reporting, and encourages richer inferential practice using context, estimation, transparency, and complementary methods.
Aim: To improve scientific inference by correcting common misuses and misinterpretations of p-values and statistical significance, and by encouraging fuller reporting, contextual interpretation, and consideration of alternative inferential approaches.

Scope.Coverage: Any empirical manuscript that reports p-values, statistical significance, or hypothesis tests, especially work where inferential claims may otherwise be reduced to threshold-based interpretation.
Scope.Focus: Interpretation of p-values, distinction between model incompatibility and truth claims, avoidance of mechanical significance thresholds, full reporting of analyses, emphasis on effect size and importance rather than significance alone, and the limited evidential value of p-values in isolation.
Scope.ContrastWith: This is not a study-design-specific checklist and not a replacement for field-specific reporting standards such as CONSORT, STROBE, or TRIPOD. It is a cross-cutting inferential overlay for how p-values and significance claims are interpreted and reported.

EvidenceExamplesPositive: A manuscript reports multiple p-values and significance claims, and needs guidance on thresholding, selective reporting, and the distinction between significance and practical importance.

A quantitative study uses null-hypothesis significance testing and would benefit from explicit reminders that p-values are not probabilities that the null is true and should be supplemented with effect estimates and uncertainty.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A purely qualitative manuscript with no statistical inference or significance testing is not a meaningful match for the ASA p-values statement.
A paper that reports only descriptive summaries and no p-values, significance tests, or inferential thresholds is unlikely to need this overlay.
Matching.Explain: Use this ASA overlay for manuscripts that report p-values or significance tests and need guidance on correct interpretation, transparency of analysis, and avoiding threshold-driven overclaiming.

Name: BARG
LongName: Bayesian Analysis Reporting Guidelines
Description: BARG is a reporting guideline for Bayesian analyses that aims to improve the quality, transparency, and reproducibility of contemporary Bayesian work. It covers model specification, computation, posterior description, decision criteria, sensitivity analysis, and reproducible sharing of code and data.
Aim: To help researchers report Bayesian analyses in a way that makes the model, computation, inferential decisions, sensitivity to priors, and reproducibility resources transparent and evaluable.

Scope.Coverage: Empirical manuscripts that rely on Bayesian statistical analysis, especially contemporary applications using MCMC, prior specification, model comparison, posterior inference, and reproducible code or workflow sharing.
Scope.Focus: Motivation for using Bayesian methods, formal model specification, prior justification, computation diagnostics, posterior summaries and predictive checks, decision criteria for null values or model comparison, sensitivity to priors, and public reproducibility materials.
Scope.ContrastWith: BARG is a methods-specific reporting standard for Bayesian analysis, not a study-design-specific checklist. It complements design-specific guides such as CONSORT, STROBE, PRISMA, or TRIPOD when Bayesian methods are central to the analysis.

Aliases: Nature Human Behaviour Bayesian reporting guidelines
EvidenceExamplesPositive: A paper reports Bayesian parameter estimation with priors, credible intervals, MCMC diagnostics, posterior predictive checks, and public code or data.
A clinical or behavioral-science study compares Bayesian models with Bayes factors and needs explicit reporting of priors, sensitivity analysis, and reproducible scripts.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A purely frequentist paper that reports only p-values and confidence intervals without Bayesian modeling is not a BARG manuscript.
A qualitative or non-statistical paper with no posterior distributions, priors, or Bayesian decisions should not map to BARG.
Matching.Explain: Use BARG for manuscripts where Bayesian analysis is central and transparent reporting of the model, computation, posterior inference, sensitivity analysis, and reproducibility materials is needed.

Name: CARE
LongName: The CARE (CAse REport) Guidelines: Consensus-based Clinical Case Reporting Guideline Development
Description: The CARE Guidelines provide a consensus-based reporting framework for clinical case reports to improve completeness, transparency, and usefulness. The checklist outlines essential information to include when reporting individual patient cases in clinical medicine.
Aim: To improve the accuracy, transparency, and completeness of published clinical case reports by providing a standardized checklist of essential reporting elements.

Scope.Coverage: Reports describing the clinical course of an individual patient, including presentation, diagnosis, intervention, and outcomes across medical disciplines.
Scope.Focus: Structured and transparent reporting of patient information, clinical findings, diagnostic assessment, therapeutic interventions, follow-up, outcomes, and patient perspective in single case reports.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not intended for randomized trials (CONSORT), observational analytic studies (STROBE), systematic reviews (PRISMA), or formal case series with comparative statistical analysis.

EvidenceExamplesPositive: A rare adverse drug reaction: a case report
CARE guideline case report
EvidenceExamplesNegative: Randomized controlled trial of...
Systematic review and meta-analysis of...
Matching.Explain: Routes single-patient clinical case reports to the CARE guideline. Excludes trials, observational analytic studies, and evidence syntheses.

Name: CEE
LongName: CEE-ENEV Checklist for Editors Assessing Validity of Systematic Reviews in the Environmental Sector
Description: A practical editor/reviewer checklist from the Collaboration for Environmental Evidence (CEE) and Environmental Evidence for quickly assessing whether manuscripts claiming to be systematic reviews meet core conduct and reporting expectations.
Aim: To support editors and peer reviewers in identifying manuscripts that do or do not qualify as systematic reviews in the environmental sector, based on transparent and replicable methods.

Scope.Coverage: Reviews in the environmental sector that claim systematic-review status and need rapid validity checks for core conduct and reporting requirements.
Scope.Focus: Protocol availability, full methodological transparency, reproducible searching and screening, explicit critical appraisal, transparent extraction and synthesis, and clear discussion of review and evidence-base limitations.
Scope.ContrastWith: This is a discipline-specific editorial validity checklist for environmental systematic reviews. It complements broad systematic-review reporting guidance by providing rapid screening criteria for environmental evidence journals and reviewers.

EvidenceExamplesPositive: This environmental evidence synthesis provides protocol registration, full replicable search strings, explicit eligibility criteria, and transparent screening counts.
The review reports study-level critical appraisal and provides extracted data tables enabling replication of synthesis.
Authors justify the synthesis approach and explicitly discuss limitations and risk of bias in the evidence base.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: The manuscript is a narrative environmental review with no protocol, no replicable search strings, and no transparent screening workflow.
This paper labels itself a systematic review but omits critical appraisal and extracted data needed for synthesis replication.
A biomedical SR paper with no environmental evidence context is out of scope for this checklist.
Matching.Explain: Adds a discipline-specific environmental systematic-review validity checklist for editorial/reviewer triage of manuscripts that claim SR status in environmental evidence contexts.

Name: CERT
LongName: Consensus on Exercise Reporting Template (CERT): Explanation and Elaboration Statement
Description: CERT is a 16-item exercise-intervention reporting checklist (with sub-items) to improve completeness, reproducibility, and implementation usefulness of reported exercise programs.
Aim: To improve how exercise interventions are reported by ensuring clear detail on equipment, provider qualifications, delivery, progression, tailoring, dosage, setting, and adherence/fidelity.

Scope.Coverage: Manuscripts that evaluate or describe exercise interventions, including clinical and public-health exercise programs delivered in supervised, unsupervised, individual, group, or home-based settings.
Scope.Focus: Detailed exercise intervention reporting: equipment, provider qualifications, delivery mode, adherence measurement, motivation strategies, progression rules, exercise content, home components, non-exercise components, adverse events, dosage, tailoring, starting level, and planned/actual fidelity.
Scope.ContrastWith: CERT is a method-specific reporting overlay for exercise interventions and should be used alongside the primary design guideline for the study type (for example CONSORT for randomized trials).

EvidenceExamplesPositive: The exercise intervention is reported using CERT with detailed progression rules, dosage, adherence, and fidelity metrics.
We provide full exercise prescription details, home program components, provider training, and adverse-event reporting.
This randomized exercise trial reports individual tailoring and starting-level decision rules per CERT items.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: The manuscript discusses digital certificates and encrypted communication, not exercise intervention reporting.
This pharmacology trial has no exercise intervention content, progression, or exercise-dose reporting.
The article is a policy commentary without intervention delivery or adherence details.
Matching.Explain: Adds an exercise-specific reporting overlay when manuscripts include exercise interventions and require reproducible reporting of delivery, progression, dosage, tailoring, and fidelity.

Name: CHAMP
LongName: CHecklist for Statistical Assessment of Medical Papers
Description: A 30-item checklist for statistical assessment of medical papers. CHAMP is designed primarily for editors and peer reviewers assessing the design, conduct, data analysis, reporting, presentation, and interpretation of medical research manuscripts, while also serving as a reference for authors and readers.
Aim: Improve the statistical validity and reporting quality of medical research papers by guiding reviewers, editors, authors, and readers through common problems in design, analysis, reporting, and interpretation.

Scope.Coverage: General medical and sports-science research papers that require a broad statistical assessment of design, analysis, reporting, and interpretation.
Scope.Focus: Research goal and study design, sample size, protocol consistency, statistical methods and assumptions, treatment effects and confounding, missing data, numerical presentation, meta-analysis utility, figure and table quality, and interpretation discipline.
Scope.ContrastWith: CHAMP is a statistical appraisal checklist rather than a design-specific reporting standard. It complements study-type guidelines such as CONSORT, STROBE, PRISMA, or TRIPOD by focusing on common statistical-review issues across many medical paper types.

Aliases: CHecklist for statistical Assessment of Medical Papers
EvidenceExamplesPositive: An editor or peer reviewer is assessing a medical manuscript for common statistical problems spanning design, confounding, missing data, reporting, and interpretation.
A clinical or epidemiologic paper needs a broad statistical-review checklist rather than a narrow design-specific reporting statement.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A manuscript that only needs a design-specific reporting standard such as CONSORT or PRISMA, without a broader statistical appraisal overlay, is not a pure CHAMP use case.
A purely theoretical paper with no empirical design, statistical analysis, or numerical reporting would not meaningfully match CHAMP.
Matching.Explain: Use CHAMP when a medical paper needs structured statistical appraisal across design, analysis, reporting, and interpretation, especially in editorial or peer-review contexts.

Name: CHEERS
LongName: Consolidated Health Economic Evaluation Reporting Standards 2022 (CHEERS 2022) Statement
Description: CHEERS 2022 provides a 28-item reporting checklist for health economic evaluations to improve transparency and completeness of reporting so that studies are identifiable, interpretable, and useful for decision making.
Aim: To improve the transparency, completeness, and usefulness of reporting of health economic evaluations of interventions for policy and practice decision making.

Scope.Coverage: Reporting of health economic evaluations of health interventions, including analyses that consider costs alone and analyses that consider both costs and consequences, across healthcare and related decision contexts.
Scope.Focus: Checklist items covering identification and summary, decision context, analytic plan, population/setting/comparators, perspective and time horizon, discounting, outcomes and valuation, costs and resource valuation, modelling and assumptions, heterogeneity/distributional effects/uncertainty, patient and stakeholder engagement, results presentation, discussion of implications and limitations, and disclosures.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not a tool to score study quality or judge whether methods were appropriate; it is a minimum reporting standard for describing what was done and found in an economic evaluation.

Aliases: Consolidated Health Economic Evaluation Reporting Standards
Consolidated Health Economic Evaluation Reporting Standards 2022
EvidenceExamplesPositive: We conducted a cost-effectiveness analysis comparing intervention A versus usual care and report according to CHEERS 2022.
This economic evaluation reports costs, QALYs, an ICER, and probabilistic sensitivity analysis (CHEERS 2022).
EvidenceExamplesNegative: We report a randomized controlled trial according to CONSORT (no economic evaluation).
This is a systematic review and meta-analysis reported according to PRISMA (not an economic evaluation report).
Matching.Explain: Routes manuscripts that are health economic evaluations to CHEERS 2022 based on explicit mentions of CHEERS or common economic evaluation terminology and outputs (e.g., QALYs, ICER).

Name: CONSORT
LongName: CONSORT 2025 statement: updated guideline for reporting randomised trials
Description: CONSORT 2025 provides an updated minimum set of reporting items for randomised trial reports, aiming to make methods and results clear, complete, and transparent. It includes a 30-item checklist (with sub-items) and related guidance to help authors, editors, and reviewers assess whether key information about design, conduct, analysis, and findings is reported.
Aim: To improve the completeness and transparency of reporting of randomised trials so that readers can appraise validity, interpret findings, and use results in practice and research.

Scope.Coverage: Completed randomized controlled trials (RCTs) in humans, focusing primarily on the common two-group, parallel-arm trial design【15†】. It applies exclusively to manuscripts reporting final trial results of prospectively randomized interventions【16†】.
Scope.Focus: Provides an evidence-based, minimum set of items for transparent and complete trial reporting. CONSORT emphasizes clear description of trial design, participant flow, interventions, randomization and blinding procedures, outcomes, and statistical results【16†】. This enables readers and reviewers to appraise the trial’s validity and reproducibility.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not for trial protocols (use SPIRIT for protocol papers) or other study types. It excludes observational studies and case reports (use STROBE or related guidelines for those), and not for systematic reviews (use PRISMA). In short, CONSORT is specific to RCT results, whereas trial protocols, observational research, and reviews each have their own specialized checklists.

EvidenceExamplesPositive: We conducted a randomized controlled trial and report it according to the CONSORT statement.
Participants were randomly assigned 1:1; allocation concealment and blinding are described.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: This is a trial protocol and will be reported according to SPIRIT.
We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized trials (PRISMA).
This observational cohort study is reported according to STROBE.
Matching.Explain: Maps completed randomized controlled trial reports to CONSORT while avoiding protocols, observational studies, case reports, and evidence syntheses.

Name: DA-RT
LongName: Data Access and Research Transparency (DA-RT): A Joint Statement by Political Science Journal Editors
Description: DA-RT is a political-science transparency overlay that emphasizes data access, restricted-data disclosure, analytic transparency, long-term access to analytic materials, and formal data citation for manuscripts making evidence-based claims.
Aim: To improve the credibility, evaluability, and reproducibility of political science research by requiring clear access to cited data, transparent analytic procedures, availability of relevant analytic materials, and proper citation of datasets as scholarly products.

Scope.Coverage: Political science and closely related social-science manuscripts that make evidence-based claims and therefore should disclose how supporting data and analytic materials can be accessed, assessed, and cited.
Scope.Focus: Trusted-repository data access, handling of restricted data, transparent description of analytic procedures, long-term availability of analytic materials, and complete dataset citation.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not a study-design-specific checklist and not a substitute for trial, observational, qualitative, or systematic-review reporting standards. The core 2015 joint statement is a transparency-policy overlay rather than a full manuscript-structure guide, so purely formal-theory papers may only partially match unless a journal extends DA-RT-style requirements to proofs or computation.

Aliases: political science journal editors transparency statement
EvidenceExamplesPositive: The manuscript states that underlying survey and replication data are deposited in a trusted repository, provides a DOI, and cites each external dataset with version information.
A political science fieldwork article explains restricted interview data, documents the exemption pathway, and shares codebooks and redacted analytic materials through a long-term archive.
The paper references DA-RT explicitly and includes data access, analytic transparency, and dataset citation statements.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A purely theoretical paper with no evidentiary materials, no computation, and no data access discussion may only weakly match DA-RT.
A biomedical trial manuscript follows CONSORT and contains no political science or DA-RT-style transparency context.
A simulation methods paper discusses code release but has no social-science transparency framing or evidentiary data access requirements.
Matching.Explain: Apply DA-RT as a social-science transparency overlay when a manuscript, especially in political science, presents evidence-based claims and should disclose data access, analytic transparency, long-term access to materials, and dataset citation practices.

Name: Experiments
LongName: Reporting Guidelines for Experimental Research: A Report from the Experimental Research Section Standards Committee
Description: A reporting guideline for laboratory, field, and survey experiments in social science, designed to improve transparency and evaluability of experimental design, implementation, attrition, and analysis reporting.
Aim: To ensure experimental manuscripts report enough methodological and results detail for readers and reviewers to evaluate validity, interpret findings, and assess replicability.

Scope.Coverage: Social science manuscripts reporting randomized or quasi-randomized experiments, especially political science lab, field, and survey experiments with treatment and control groups.
Scope.Focus: Transparent reporting of hypotheses, participant recruitment and context, assignment procedures, treatment delivery, participant flow, attrition, and intent-to-treat statistical summaries.
Scope.ContrastWith: This guide is a social-science experiment reporting framework and not a medical trial standard, qualitative reporting guide, or non-experimental observational reporting standard.

Aliases: Gerber Arceneaux Boudreau Dowling Hillygus Palfrey Biggers Hendry 2014
EvidenceExamplesPositive: We conducted a survey experiment in which respondents were randomly assigned to one of three message treatments and a control condition; response rates and assignment procedures are reported in detail.
The manuscript reports unit of randomization, baseline covariate means by treatment group, CONSORT-style participant flow, and ITT outcome summaries.
A laboratory experiment paper describes blocking, manipulation checks, treatment fidelity, and attrition by assigned group, with reasons for exclusions from analysis.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A randomized clinical trial in cardiology that follows CONSORT and does not involve social science lab/field/survey experimental context.
A non-experimental observational social survey without treatment/control assignment.
An engineering materials paper describing stress-testing experiments unrelated to social-science treatment assignment reporting.
Matching.Explain: Use this as a social-science experimental reporting overlay when manuscripts report field/lab/survey randomized experiments with treatment-control design and detailed assignment, participant-flow, and ITT reporting signals.

Name: GATHER
LongName: Guidelines for Accurate and Transparent Health Estimates Reporting (GATHER) statement
Description: GATHER defines minimum reporting requirements for studies that produce quantitative population-level health estimates by synthesizing multiple data sources with statistical or mathematical models.
Aim: To improve transparency, interpretability, and reproducibility of global health estimates by requiring explicit reporting of objectives, data inputs, analytical methods, uncertainty, results dissemination, and limitations.
Keywords: Guidelines for Accurate and Transparent Health Estimates Reporting

Scope.Coverage: Studies that estimate health indicators across multiple populations, places, or time periods using multiple input datasets and formal synthesis/modeling methods.
Scope.Focus: Transparent reporting of indicator definitions, population/time coverage, data identification and selection, input-data characteristics and bias, model specification and selection, uncertainty quantification, access to input data and analytical code, and interpretation of estimate updates.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not intended for single-source descriptive reports (for example one survey round without multi-source synthesis), clinical trial reports, or general epidemiologic analyses that do not generate comparative population-level health estimates via modeling.

Aliases: Guidelines for Accurate and Transparent Health Estimates Reporting
EvidenceExamplesPositive: We estimated national and subnational under-5 mortality from 1990-2024 by synthesizing multiple survey and vital-registration sources, reporting inclusion criteria, model selection, uncertainty intervals, and code access.

This analysis provides age-sex-country prevalence estimates using a Bayesian hierarchical model with explicit data-source metadata, ad-hoc exclusions, sensitivity analyses, and downloadable estimate files.
Updated global burden estimates are presented with reasons for changes from prior rounds, detailed input-data processing, and transparent uncertainty propagation across modeled components.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A randomized trial compares two hypertension treatments and reports participant outcomes without multi-source population estimation methods.
A single hospital cross-sectional survey reports local prevalence at one timepoint with no multi-population modeling or uncertainty framework for global estimates.
A qualitative policy paper discusses national health planning and data gathering challenges but does not produce quantitative modeled health estimates.
Matching.Explain: Use this as the primary design guide for studies producing model-based population health estimates across locations or time from multiple data inputs, emphasizing transparent inputs, methods, uncertainty, and reproducibility artifacts.

Name: Geoscientific model development
LongName: The publication of geoscientific model developments (GMD editorial framework, code/data policy, and review criteria)
Description: A geoscientific-model reporting framework derived from GMD editorial policy, manuscript-type framework, code/data policy, publication guide, and review criteria. It emphasizes model traceability, reproducibility, code/data accessibility, and rigorous evaluation.
Aim: To ensure manuscripts describing geoscientific model developments are reproducible, traceable, technically rigorous, and review-ready, with explicit code/data availability and transparent model-version reporting.
Keywords: model name and version in title

Scope.Coverage: Manuscripts in geoscientific numerical modeling that describe models, model developments, assessment methods, experiment protocols, and model-evaluation studies under GMD-style editorial requirements.
Scope.Focus: Model/version traceability, code/data availability, reproducible model and method description, benchmark/observation evaluation, clear manuscript classification, and editorial-quality checks at submission and review stages.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not intended for general geoscience result papers that do not primarily describe modelling methodology, model development, experiment protocols, or reproducibility-relevant computational details.

EvidenceExamplesPositive: This model description paper includes model name/version in the title, a mandatory code-availability section, repository DOI for the release, benchmark and observational evaluation, and explicit discussion of scope and limitations.
Our GMD technical-development manuscript documents data assimilation code changes, provides reproducible test cases, and reports verification-versus-evaluation evidence for model updates.
The submitted MIP protocol paper provides experiment and boundary-condition versioning, data/code availability statements, and reproducible protocol details for independent models.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A geophysics paper reports observational findings only, without model description, versioning, or code/data availability requirements.
A medical manuscript containing the acronym GMD discusses clinical outcomes and has no geoscientific modelling workflow.
A narrative climate-policy commentary references models broadly but provides no computational methods, reproducibility details, or model-version traceability.
Matching.Explain: Use as a geoscience discipline overlay when a manuscript presents geoscientific model development or model-related methods/protocol work and should satisfy explicit GMD-style traceability, reproducibility, and code/data-access publication requirements.

Name: GUIDED
LongName: Guidance for reporting intervention development studies in health research (GUIDED)
Description: GUIDED is a 14-item reporting checklist for intervention development studies in health research, aimed at improving transparency, consistency, and learning from intervention-development processes.
Aim: To improve quality and consistency of reporting of health intervention development studies by covering context, purpose, target population, development approaches, evidence and theory use, stakeholder input, adaptation, uncertainty, and open accessibility.

Scope.Coverage: Health and healthcare manuscripts reporting how interventions were developed before or alongside feasibility/evaluation, including evidence synthesis, theory use, co-design or stakeholder-informed approaches.
Scope.Focus: Transparent reporting of development rationale, methods, evidence inputs, theoretical basis, stakeholder roles, design changes over time, subgroup considerations, residual uncertainties, and accessibility of the development report.
Scope.ContrastWith: GUIDED focuses on intervention development process reporting, not full trial-result reporting. It complements design-specific guidelines (for example CONSORT) and intervention-description guidance (for example TIDieR).

Aliases: Guidance for reporting intervention development studies
GUIDance for the rEporting of intervention Development
EvidenceExamplesPositive: The study reports context, purpose, target population, and how evidence and theory informed intervention development decisions.
Stakeholder contributions are described alongside iterative changes in intervention content and unresolved uncertainties.
Authors explicitly use GUIDED and link intervention description to TIDieR reporting.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: The manuscript reports trial outcomes only and does not describe how the intervention was developed.
This is a pure software engineering design paper with no health intervention development context.
The article provides intervention results but no account of evidence/theory/stakeholder inputs used during development.
Matching.Explain: Adds an intervention-development reporting overlay when manuscripts describe how health interventions were designed and iteratively refined before or alongside evaluation.

Name: ICMJE Recommendations
LongName: Recommendations for the Conduct, Reporting, Editing, and Publication of Scholarly Work in Medical Journals
Description: The ICMJE Recommendations provide a broad, cross-cutting framework for ethical conduct, transparent reporting, manuscript preparation, peer review, and publication practices in medical journals. This checklist operationalizes the manuscript-preparation guidance (Section IV.A) as discrete, manuscript-detectable information items that an LLM can classify as present or absent in the submitted text, together with the manuscript-detectable disclosure, ethics, and transparency statements drawn from the wider Recommendations.
Subdomain: Medical journal reporting and publication ethics
Aim: To help authors, editors, reviewers, and journals produce accurate, clear, reproducible, and unbiased medical-journal articles by specifying minimum expectations for manuscript preparation, disclosure, participant protection, clinical-trial transparency, and publication integrity.

Scope.Coverage: Manuscripts submitted to medical journals, especially human and animal health research reports, where authors need to meet baseline expectations for ethical conduct, transparent reporting, manuscript structure, disclosure, and publication integrity.
Scope.Focus: IMRAD manuscript structure; title page and structured abstract content; clear description of participants, sex/gender and race/ethnicity, methods, and statistics; logical results reporting; responsible discussion; reference practice; tables, figures, units, and abbreviations; and the disclosure, ethics, trial-transparency, and data-sharing statements that can be detected within the manuscript text.
Scope.ContrastWith: This is a broad editorial and reporting framework rather than a design-specific checklist. It complements specialist study-design standards such as CONSORT, STROBE, PRISMA, and STARD instead of replacing them. Submission-process requirements that cannot be verified from the manuscript text (for example, cover-letter statements, co-author approval, or corresponding-author attestations) are intentionally excluded from this checklist.

Aliases: Recommendations for the Conduct, Reporting, Editing, and Publication of Scholarly Work in Medical Journals
EvidenceExamplesPositive: A clinical manuscript prepared for journal submission reports authorship roles, trial registration, conflicts disclosures, participant protection, structured abstract content, and a data-sharing statement.
A medical-journal submission describes IRB approval, sponsor role, statistical methods, funding, and AI-assisted writing disclosure under ICMJE-style editorial requirements.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A basic-science manuscript that only needs a narrow assay-specific reporting checklist and does not involve medical-journal publication expectations is not a strong ICMJE match on its own.
A purely internal memo or conference slide deck with no journal-style manuscript structure, disclosures, or publication context should not map to ICMJE.
Matching.Explain: Use ICMJE as a broad medical-journal reporting and publication-ethics overlay when manuscripts need baseline expectations for authorship, disclosure, participant protection, trial transparency, manuscript preparation, and responsible AI use.

Name: JARS-Advanced Quantitative Methods
LongName: JARS-Quant Tables 7 and 8: Reporting Standards for Structural Equation Modeling and Bayesian Techniques
Description: This JARS-Quant add-on captures reporting expectations for two complex quantitative-analysis families in psychology: structural equation modeling and Bayesian techniques. It extends the base quantitative table with method-specific reporting detail.
Aim: To improve interpretability and reproducibility of complex psychological analyses by requiring explicit reporting of SEM specification, estimation, fit, and respecification as well as Bayesian model, prior, posterior, computation, and decision details.

Scope.Coverage: Psychology and behavioral-science manuscripts that rely on structural equation modeling or Bayesian techniques and therefore need more detailed reporting than standard inferential summaries alone provide.
Scope.Focus: SEM model description, sample-size rationale, missing-data and distribution diagnostics, model identification, estimation and fit, respecification, parameter estimates, and Bayesian model specification, priors, posteriors, computations, fit checks, Bayes factors, and model averaging.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not a stand-alone design guide for all studies; these methods-specific add-ons are used with the JARS-Quant base table and relevant design modules when SEM or Bayesian approaches are central to the analysis.

EvidenceExamplesPositive: A psychology manuscript reports SEM fit statistics, identification, respecification, and standardized and unstandardized estimates.
A Bayesian paper describes priors, posterior HPD intervals, MCMC settings, fit checks, and Bayes factors.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A simple t-test paper with no SEM or Bayesian methods should not map to this module.
A meta-analysis without Bayesian modeling belongs with MARS or another synthesis standard instead.
Matching.Explain: Use this add-on when SEM or Bayesian techniques are central enough that method-specific reporting detail is required.

Name: JARS-Experimental
LongName: JARS-Quant Table 2: Reporting Standards for Studies With an Experimental Manipulation
Description: The JARS-Quant experimental table provides psychology-specific reporting expectations for studies with an experimental manipulation, including randomized and nonrandomized assignments and additional clinical-trial reporting modules.
Aim: To ensure psychology experiments and related trials report manipulations, allocation, masking, flow, fidelity, analytic methods, and trial-specific protocol details clearly enough for evaluation and replication.

Scope.Coverage: Psychology and behavioral-science studies with an experimental manipulation, including randomized and nonrandomized allocation and trials involving clinical or health-related interventions.
Scope.Focus: Manipulation details, delivery and analysis units, participant flow, treatment fidelity, baseline data, adverse events, randomization or assignment procedures, masking, and clinical-trial registration and protocol reporting.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not for purely observational designs, longitudinal studies without manipulation, or research syntheses. This table is meant to be used together with the JARS-Quant master table.

Aliases: Reporting Standards for Studies With an Experimental Manipulation
EvidenceExamplesPositive: A psychology experiment reports manipulation delivery, unit of randomization, masking, participant flow, and treatment fidelity under APA reporting standards.
A behavioral clinical trial reports registration, protocol availability, assessor masking, and trial-specific intervention details.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A nonmanipulated correlational study should use the observational JARS module instead of the experimental one.
A meta-analysis with no experimental manipulation is not a JARS-Experimental match.
Matching.Explain: Use this overlay for psychology manuscripts with an experimental manipulation, including randomized, nonrandomized, and clinical-trial variants.

Name: JARS-Longitudinal
LongName: JARS-Quant Table 4: Reporting Standards for Longitudinal Studies
Description: The JARS-Quant longitudinal table adds reporting expectations specific to studies that observe the same unit on the same response variables across multiple occasions.
Aim: To improve the reporting of longitudinal studies by specifying expectations for wave-by-wave sample description, attrition, contextual changes, timing, missing data, analytic assumptions, and overlap with prior publications.

Scope.Coverage: Psychology and related behavioral or educational studies that repeatedly observe the same unit over time or across prespecified occasions.
Scope.Focus: Reporting of sample characteristics, recruitment and retention, attrition, contextual changes over time, timing of waves, missing-data handling, longitudinal analytic assumptions, and prior publication overlap.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not intended for single-session within-subject designs, multivariate-outcome designs without repeated measurement of the same attribute, or time-series experiments with distinct standards. This module is used together with the relevant JARS-Quant base design.

EvidenceExamplesPositive: A developmental psychology paper reports wave-by-wave attrition, contextual changes, years of data collection, and overlap with earlier publications.
A longitudinal educational study explains missing-data handling and analytic assumptions alongside repeated observations of the same participants.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A single-session within-subject experiment is not a JARS-Longitudinal match.
A meta-analysis with no repeated participant observations should not map to this module.
Matching.Explain: Use this module for psychology manuscripts that repeatedly observe the same unit across multiple occasions and need longitudinal-specific reporting.

Name: JARS-Observational
LongName: JARS-Quant Table 3: Reporting Standards for Studies Using No Experimental Manipulation
Description: The JARS-Quant observational table adapts psychology-specific reporting expectations for nonexperimental studies, including single-group designs, natural-group comparisons, and observational or correlational studies.
Aim: To improve the reporting of observational and other nonexperimental psychology studies by clarifying expectations for design description, participant selection, variables, comparability of assessment, analysis, and limitations.

Scope.Coverage: Psychology and behavioral-science studies that do not involve an experimental manipulation, including single-group analyses and comparisons across naturally occurring groups.
Scope.Focus: Study design, data type, participant selection, data sources, variable definition and measurement, comparability across groups, confounder handling, and study limitations.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not for manipulated experiments, clinical trials, or research syntheses. This module supplements the JARS-Quant master table for nonexperimental designs.

Aliases: Reporting Standards for Studies Using No Experimental Manipulation
EvidenceExamplesPositive: A psychology observational study reports participant selection, record-linkage rules, confounders, and comparability of assessment using the JARS observational module.
A natural-group comparison paper explicitly cites JARS Table 3 and discusses misclassification and unmeasured confounding.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A randomized experiment with treatment delivery details should map to JARS-Experimental instead.
A research synthesis with no primary participants is not a JARS-Observational match.
Matching.Explain: Use this module for psychology manuscripts with no experimental manipulation, especially observational, correlational, archival, or natural-group comparison designs.

Name: JARS-Qual
LongName: Journal Article Reporting Standards for Qualitative Research
Description: JARS-Qual is APA's reporting standard for manuscripts that report primary qualitative research in psychology and related social sciences. It emphasizes qualitative framing, reflexive researcher positioning, detailed source and recruitment description, transparent data collection and analysis, methodological integrity, grounded findings, and discussion of transferability and implications.
Aim: To improve the clarity, methodological integrity, transparency, and usefulness of primary qualitative research reports by specifying the information that should be included across title, abstract, introduction, method, findings, and discussion.
ManuscriptType: Behavioral or social science qualitative manuscript

Scope.Coverage: Primary qualitative research reports in psychology and related behavioral, social, and educational sciences, including studies based on interviews, observations, documents, archives, and other qualitative data sources.
Scope.Focus: Approach to inquiry, problem framing, researcher reflexivity, participant or source description, recruitment and selection, data collection and recording, analytic strategies, methodological integrity, findings presentation, transferability, and implications.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not a quantitative reporting guide, not a mixed-methods integration standard, and not a qualitative meta-analysis standard. JARS-Qual is designed for primary qualitative studies and should be distinguished from QMARS and MMARS, which address other evidence structures.

Aliases: Journal Article Reporting Standards for Qualitative Research
EvidenceExamplesPositive: A qualitative interview study reports researcher reflexivity, purposive sampling, iterative data collection, thematic analysis, and discussion of transferability under APA qualitative reporting standards.
A primary qualitative manuscript describes participants or archives, approach to inquiry, methodological integrity, grounded findings, and ethical challenges.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A quantitative experiment with effect sizes and random assignment is not a JARS-Qual manuscript.
A qualitative metasynthesis belongs with QMARS rather than the primary qualitative-research table.
Matching.Explain: Use JARS-Qual for primary qualitative research manuscripts that need APA-style reporting of reflexivity, sources, recruitment, data collection, qualitative analysis, methodological integrity, findings, and transferability.

Name: JARS-Quant
LongName: Journal Article Reporting Standards for Quantitative Research in Psychology
Description: APA's JARS-Quant master standard for reporting quantitative empirical studies in psychology and related behavioral, social, and educational sciences. It provides a core table of reporting expectations that applies regardless of design, with additional design-specific modules used alongside it.
Aim: To improve the clarity, completeness, transparency, and reproducibility of quantitative research reports in psychology by specifying the core information that should be reported across article sections regardless of specific study design.

Scope.Coverage: Quantitative empirical studies in psychology and related behavioral, social, and educational sciences, especially manuscripts prepared in APA style that may also require design-specific companion tables.
Scope.Focus: Core reporting of title, abstract, introduction, method, results, and discussion elements including hypotheses, sampling, measurement, design classification, data diagnostics, statistical analysis, participant flow, interpretation, and generalizability.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not a qualitative reporting guide and not a stand-alone replacement for the design-specific JARS modules, SEM/Bayesian add-ons, or meta-analysis standards. JARS-Quant serves as the universal quantitative base table to be combined with more specific modules where needed.

Aliases: Journal Article Reporting Standards for Quantitative Research in Psychology
EvidenceExamplesPositive: The manuscript cites JARS-Quant and separates primary, secondary, and exploratory hypotheses while reporting participant flow and data diagnostics.
An APA-style psychology article reports sample selection, psychometrics, masking, data diagnostics, and effect sizes in the core JARS-Quant structure.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A qualitative interview study that follows SRQR but has no quantitative analyses is not a JARS-Quant match.
A pure meta-analysis manuscript should map more directly to MARS than to the JARS-Quant base table.
Matching.Explain: Use JARS-Quant as the master psychology quantitative-reporting overlay when a manuscript presents original quantitative empirical data and follows APA-style reporting expectations.

Name: JARS-REC
LongName: Journal Article Reporting Standards for Race, Ethnicity, and Culture
Description: JARS-REC is APA's cross-cutting reporting standard for describing race, ethnicity, and culture responsibly and transparently in psychology research. It supplements existing APA JARS modules by adding expectations for conceptual framing, sampling, measurement, analysis, interpretation, generalizability, and citation practice related to race, ethnicity, and culture.
Subdomain: Psychology race, ethnicity, and culture reporting
Aim: To improve the reporting of race, ethnicity, and culture in psychology manuscripts by requiring authors to define these constructs clearly, situate them in local sociocultural context, avoid deficit framing and racial essentialism, report sample and measurement decisions transparently, and discuss interpretation, generalizability, and citation practice in equity-aware ways.

Scope.Coverage: Psychology and related manuscripts that report research involving race, ethnicity, and culture, including studies where these constructs are focal research topics, moderators, sample descriptors, analytic covariates, or important contextual features.
Scope.Focus: Clear construct definition, local context, theory selection, sample description, measurement validity across groups, recruitment and representation, analysis choices involving race and ethnicity, contextualized interpretation, constraints on generality, implications for equity, and equitable citation practice.
Scope.ContrastWith: This is a cross-cutting reporting overlay rather than a design-specific methods checklist. It complements APA's quantitative, qualitative, and mixed-methods JARS standards when race, ethnicity, and culture are relevant to the manuscript.

Aliases: APA race ethnicity and culture reporting standards
Journal Article Reporting Standards for Race, Ethnicity, and Culture
EvidenceExamplesPositive: A psychology manuscript examines mental health across racial and ethnic groups, explains how categories were measured, justifies the theoretical framework for the studied communities, and discusses structural racism in interpreting results.

A mixed methods study reports recruitment language, participant country of origin and home language, subgroup measurement validity, and limits on generalizing findings across culturally distinct populations.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A manuscript mentions sample demographics briefly but does not analyze or discuss race, ethnicity, culture, or related contextual issues in any substantive way.
A laboratory methods paper with no human participants and no discussion of race, ethnicity, culture, or equity concerns should not map to JARS-REC.
Matching.Explain: Use JARS-REC as a cross-cutting APA overlay when manuscripts need explicit, equity-aware reporting of race, ethnicity, and culture in framing, sampling, measurement, analysis, interpretation, generalizability, or citation practice.

Name: JARS-Replication
LongName: JARS-Quant Table 6: Reporting Standards for Replication Studies
Description: The JARS-Quant replication table provides reporting expectations for external replication studies in psychology, emphasizing explicit comparison with the original study's design, participants, instrumentation, and analytic criteria.
Aim: To improve the clarity and evaluability of replication reports by requiring authors to describe the type of replication and to compare the replication study directly with the original across key methodological dimensions.

Scope.Coverage: External replication studies in psychology and related fields where authors repeat one or more specific prior studies and need to compare the original and replication directly.
Scope.Focus: Replication type, deviations from the original, participant and setting comparisons, instrumentation comparability, translation and psychometrics, analytic comparability, and decision criteria for judging replication success.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not for internal cross-validation, bootstrapping, or resampling procedures within a single dataset. This module is intended for external replications of previously published or archived studies.

EvidenceExamplesPositive: A replication manuscript explicitly labels itself as a direct replication and compares recruitment, instrumentation, and decision criteria with the original study.
A psychology replication report uses original analyses plus Bayes factors and explains what counts as successful replication.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A novel experiment with no original study comparison is not a JARS-Replication paper.
Internal cross-validation or bootstrap resampling within one sample should not map here.
Matching.Explain: Use this module for psychology manuscripts that explicitly report external replications of prior studies.

Name: JARS-Single-Case
LongName: JARS-Quant Table 5: Reporting Standards for N-of-1 Studies
Description: The JARS-Quant single-case table summarizes the psychology-specific reporting expectations for N-of-1 and related single-case experimental designs, drawing on SCRIBE 2016 for behavioral intervention contexts.
Aim: To improve clarity and replicability of single-case experimental studies by specifying how design structure, procedural changes, randomization, sequence completion, and raw outcome data should be reported.

Scope.Coverage: Single-case and N-of-1 experimental studies in psychology and behavioral intervention research, especially designs such as withdrawal-reversal, multiple-baseline, alternating-treatments, and changing-criterion studies.
Scope.Focus: Design type and phase structure, procedural changes, replication planning, randomization use, completed sequence reporting, stopping reasons, and raw participant-level outcomes.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not for conventional group experiments or observational studies. This module is intended to be used alongside the JARS-Quant base table and relevant experimental modules.

EvidenceExamplesPositive: A behavioral intervention paper reports a multiple-baseline N-of-1 design, achieved phase sequence, randomization, and raw participant traces.
A single-case psychology article explicitly references SCRIBE and provides participant-level outcome data.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A standard group randomized trial is not a JARS-Single-Case manuscript.
A meta-analysis or observational cohort with no phase-based single-case design should not map here.
Matching.Explain: Use this module for psychology manuscripts reporting N-of-1 or other single-case experimental designs.

Name: Legal Data
LongName: Legal Interoperability of Research Data: Principles and Implementation Guidelines
Description: A cross-disciplinary legal and policy framework for enabling lawful, machine-readable, and harmonized access, reuse, and integration of research data across jurisdictions and institutions.
Subdomain: Research data law, policy, and governance
Aim: To improve legal interoperability of research data by promoting minimal-restriction reuse defaults, clear rights determination, balanced legal interests, transparent rights statements, harmonization mechanisms, and proper attribution and credit.
Keywords: access and reuse of research data

Scope.Coverage: Cross-domain guidance documents and policy manuscripts that define legal conditions for sharing, integrating, and reusing research data, especially in public-sector and publicly funded research contexts.
Scope.Focus: Rights determination, legal-use-condition transparency, reusable rights statements, waiver/licensing strategy, justified restrictions, harmonization across jurisdictions or institutions, and attribution/credit norms for research data reuse.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not a study-design reporting guide (for example trials, observational studies, or systematic reviews) and not a technical file-format interoperability standard by itself; it is a legal-policy overlay for data access and reuse governance.

Aliases: Legal Interoperability Principles and Implementation Guidelines
EvidenceExamplesPositive: We adopt the RDA-CODATA legal interoperability framework and specify rights statements, rights holders, and jurisdiction-aware reuse conditions for all datasets.
Our policy recommends CC0/PDDL public-domain dedication as default for publicly funded research data and limits restrictive contracts that impede downstream integration.
The guideline defines legal use conditions in machine-readable metadata so combined datasets can be reused without case-by-case authorization.
This framework balances privacy and national-security constraints with public-interest access while minimizing exclusive-use embargo periods.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: This paper proposes HL7 FHIR APIs for EHR interoperability and does not discuss research-data legal rights, licensing, or reuse governance.
The manuscript studies technical schema interoperability only, with no rights statements, legal use conditions, or cross-jurisdiction policy guidance.
A trial report includes a generic data-availability statement but no legal interoperability framework, rights-holder mapping, or harmonization recommendations.
Matching.Explain: Applies as a cross-cutting legal-policy reporting overlay when manuscripts or guidance documents address rights determination, licensing, rights statements, and harmonization needed for lawful interoperability of research data across contexts.

Name: MDAR
LongName: Materials Design Analysis Reporting Framework
Description: MDAR is a general life-sciences reporting framework that sets minimum expectations for transparent reporting of materials, study design, analysis, and reporting outputs such as data, code, and protocol information. It is intended as a cross-cutting baseline that complements more specialized discipline-specific guidelines.
Subdomain: Life sciences transparency and reproducibility reporting
Aim: To improve transparent reporting in the life sciences by requiring authors to disclose how research materials can be accessed and identified, how the study was designed and governed, how analyses and exclusions were handled, and how supporting data, code, and relevant specialist reporting standards are reported.

Scope.Coverage: Broadly applicable life-sciences manuscripts, especially exploratory laboratory and preclinical studies, where transparent reporting of materials, protocols, design safeguards, analyses, and research outputs is needed.
Scope.Focus: Accessibility, identification, and characterization of materials; preregistration and detailed protocols; experimental design safeguards such as randomization and blinding; replication and sample definition; ethics and permits; attrition and statistical methods; data and code availability; and alignment with community-specific reporting standards.
Scope.ContrastWith: MDAR is a general baseline framework rather than a specialist design-specific standard. It is meant to complement, not replace, focused guidelines such as ARRIVE, CONSORT, PRISMA, or domain-specific resource-reporting standards.

EvidenceExamplesPositive: A life-sciences manuscript reports materials availability, RRIDs for key reagents, preregistration or protocol links, randomization and blinding, technical versus biological replicates, and data and code availability statements.

A preclinical paper follows ARRIVE and also discloses ethics approvals, exclusion criteria, statistical-test justification, and repository accessions for newly generated data under the MDAR framework.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A purely theoretical manuscript with no materials, protocols, data, code, or experimental design reporting needs would not ordinarily map to MDAR.

A manuscript that only mentions one specialist checklist but provides no broader life-sciences transparency reporting on materials, design, analysis, or availability statements is not a strong MDAR match.
Matching.Explain: Use MDAR as a broad life-sciences transparency framework for manuscripts that need baseline reporting on materials, design, analysis, and reporting outputs such as data, code, protocols, and approvals.

Name: mERA
LongName: mHealth Evidence Reporting and Assessment checklist
Description: mERA is a reporting framework for digital health interventions, designed to improve transparency and comparability of mHealth program evidence by combining core digital-health reporting criteria with study-method reporting items.
Aim: To standardize reporting of digital health intervention studies so program content, context, implementation, and methodological rigor are sufficiently documented for appraisal and synthesis.

Scope.Coverage: Studies that evaluate, pilot, or implement digital health or mHealth interventions and need transparent reporting of intervention context, implementation details, and methodological quality.
Scope.Focus: Comprehensive reporting of digital intervention rationale, delivery context, study methods, participant flow, outcomes, and study quality criteria relevant to digital health evidence.
Scope.ContrastWith: mERA is a digital-health reporting overlay and should be combined with study-design-specific standards such as CONSORT or STROBE where appropriate.

EvidenceExamplesPositive: The study reports mERA-aligned digital health intervention details with methods, recruitment, outcomes, and implementation context.
Authors explicitly cite mHealth Evidence Reporting and Assessment guidance.
Telemedicine implementation outcomes are reported alongside methodological quality items.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: The manuscript is a generic software engineering paper without health intervention evidence.
The article mentions mobile apps but provides no intervention evaluation methods or outcomes.
This is a policy commentary with no study design or participant reporting.
Matching.Explain: Adds a digital-health reporting overlay when manuscripts evaluate mHealth/telemedicine interventions and need structured evidence reporting beyond design-specific checklists.

Name: Clinical Metabolomics Computational Reproducibility Checklist
LongName: A Checklist for Reproducible Computational Analysis in Clinical Metabolomics Research
Description: This checklist provides eight practical actions for making the computational aspects of clinical metabolomics studies more reproducible. It extends beyond metadata reporting alone to cover reusable data sharing, workflow versioning, software availability, containerization, hardware documentation, semantic annotation, and executable workflow delivery.
Aim: To improve reproducibility of clinical metabolomics studies by specifying concrete actions that make data reusable, computational workflows inspectable, and analyses easier for other researchers to rerun, understand, and preserve over time.

Scope.Coverage: Clinical metabolomics studies that involve computational data processing and analysis, especially studies using NMR or mass-spectrometry pipelines for biomarker discovery or translational research.
Scope.Focus: Reusable data sharing, clear metadata presentation, version-controlled workflow sharing, use of open-source downloadable tools, containerization or virtual machines, runtime hardware documentation, semantic annotation for workflow preservation, and executable or narrative workflow delivery.
Scope.ContrastWith: This checklist targets reproducible computational analysis in clinical metabolomics rather than general study design or wet-lab reporting. It complements metabolomics metadata standards and minimum reporting checklists by focusing on computational reproducibility actions.

Aliases: reproducible computational analysis in clinical metabolomics
EvidenceExamplesPositive: A clinical metabolomics study deposits raw data in MetaboLights, shares code on GitHub, documents its containerized workflow, and provides a notebook linking code, narrative, and outputs.
A biomarker discovery paper using LC-MS explains software versions, hardware requirements, and semantic workflow annotations in addition to depositing reusable metabolomics data.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A metabolomics paper that reports biological findings but provides no public data, no workflow information, and no computational reproducibility details is not a strong match for this checklist.
A non-metabolomics wet-lab study with no substantial computational workflow does not primarily map to this clinical metabolomics reproducibility guidance.
Matching.Explain: Use this checklist for clinical metabolomics studies that need explicit reporting of reusable data sharing and computational workflow reproducibility actions.

Name: MMARS
LongName: Mixed Methods Article Reporting Standards
Description: MMARS is APA's reporting standard for manuscripts that intentionally collect, analyze, and integrate qualitative and quantitative data within a mixed methods design.
Aim: To improve the reporting of mixed methods research by specifying how authors should describe mixed methods aims, design type, qualitative and quantitative strands, integration procedures, and the validity or integrity of inferences drawn across strands.

Scope.Coverage: Psychology and related social-science studies that intentionally collect, analyze, and integrate qualitative and quantitative data within a mixed methods design.
Scope.Focus: Mixed methods design identification, ordering of qualitative and quantitative aims, rationale for mixing, strand-specific sampling and analysis, integration procedures, and mixed-methods validity or legitimacy.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not for purely qualitative or purely quantitative studies. MMARS is used when a study intentionally integrates qualitative and quantitative strands to generate insights not available from either alone.

EvidenceExamplesPositive: A convergent mixed methods study reports qualitative and quantitative strands separately, uses a joint display, and explains the added value of integration.
An explanatory sequential design states qualitative, quantitative, and mixed methods aims and shows how interview findings were used to explain survey results.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A stand-alone interview study without quantitative data is not an MMARS manuscript.
A purely quantitative experiment with no integration of qualitative data should not map to MMARS.
Matching.Explain: Use MMARS for manuscripts that intentionally collect and integrate qualitative and quantitative data within a mixed methods design.

Name: Reporting Modelling Studies
LongName: Reporting guidelines for modelling studies
Description: A synthesized reporting checklist for health and population modelling studies, extracted from Table 1 of Bennett and Manuel's review of existing guidance. The paper does not introduce a formal consensus guideline, but it compiles concrete reporting items across model structure, data, uncertainty, consistency, validity, implementation, transparency, and sponsorship for population modelling manuscripts.
Aim: To improve the transparency and critical appraisal of population modelling studies by making key model design choices, data sources, assumptions, uncertainty analyses, validation steps, and implementation details explicit.

Scope.Coverage: Manuscripts that report health, disease, or policy models used to estimate outcomes over time or across populations, especially decision-analytic, simulation, and population-based disease models.
Scope.Focus: Model decision problem, perspective, structure, assumptions, comparators, data identification and incorporation, uncertainty and sensitivity analysis, calibration, internal and external consistency, predictive validity, software, transparency, and sponsorship disclosure.
Scope.ContrastWith: This is a synthesized checklist derived from a review article rather than a formal consensus statement. It is broader than a calibration-only guide and more modelling-specific than general observational or trial reporting checklists.

EvidenceExamplesPositive: A health-policy or disease-simulation manuscript reports model structure, time horizon, transition probabilities, calibration, uncertainty analysis, and validation, and needs a modelling-specific reporting checklist.
A decision-analytic or economic-evaluation model paper needs structured prompts for transparency about assumptions, data incorporation, sensitivity analysis, and software implementation.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A primary randomized trial or observational cohort report with no explicit analytic model, calibration, or sensitivity-analysis framework is not a modelling-study checklist use case.
A purely theoretical mathematical paper with no reported health-policy or population modelling application would not strongly match this checklist.
Matching.Explain: Use this checklist for health and population modelling manuscripts when the main reporting challenge is transparency about model structure, inputs, assumptions, uncertainty, validation, and implementation.

Name: Observational-Urology only
LongName: Guidelines for Reporting Observational Research in Urology: The Importance of Clear Reference to Causality
Description: A reporting guideline for observational studies in urology that focuses on how authors should frame, analyze, and discuss causality. It addresses causal language, practical implications, explicit treatment of causal questions in the introduction, methods for describing causal pathways and confounding, evaluation of residual bias, and drawing research or clinical implications that are consistent with the strength of causal inference.
Aim: To improve the reporting of observational research in urology by requiring explicit treatment of causal questions, clearer use of causal language, and better alignment between study aims, methods, interpretation, and practical recommendations.

Scope.Coverage: Observational studies in urology where authors estimate associations between exposures and outcomes and where there is any reasonable question about causality.
Scope.Focus: Explicit use of causal language, practical implications of causal claims, direct reference to causality in study aims, description of causal pathways, statistical handling of confounding and mediation, assessment of residual bias, and causal interpretation in discussion and conclusions.
