Scope.ContrastWith: Not a generic design-reporting checklist like STROBE and not a purely statistical reporting guide. This guideline specifically addresses how observational studies should talk about and evaluate causality.

Aliases: Guidelines for Reporting Observational Research in Urology
EvidenceExamplesPositive: An observational urology study claims a drug reduces risk or that a lifestyle change prevents cancer and needs guidance on whether its language and methods support causal interpretation.
A comparative effectiveness manuscript discusses confounding, propensity methods, mediation, or causal pathways and needs a causality-focused reporting overlay.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A purely descriptive case report is not the target of this guideline.
A randomized trial does not primarily need this observational-causality overlay.
Matching.Explain: Use this guideline for observational studies in urology when the manuscript touches causal questions, causal wording, confounding control, mediation, or practice recommendations that depend on more than mere association.

Name: ODD
LongName: Overview, Design concepts, and Details (ODD) protocol for describing agent-based and other simulation models
Description: ODD is a structured protocol for transparent, complete, and replicable documentation of agent-based and related simulation models, covering model purpose, entities, processes, design concepts, and implementation details.
Aim: To standardize simulation-model reporting so readers can understand model logic, assess fitness for purpose, reproduce implementations, and compare models across disciplines.

Scope.Coverage: Simulation-model manuscripts that document model structure, mechanisms, and implementation details, especially agent-based or individual-based models but also other simulation models using ODD-style structure.
Scope.Focus: Clear reporting of model purpose, represented entities and state variables, process scheduling, key design concepts, initialization, input data dependencies, and submodel formulations sufficient for replication and critical appraisal.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not intended for purely statistical analyses or non-simulation empirical studies. Complements, rather than replaces, design-specific reporting standards by adding a simulation-model documentation layer.

Aliases: The ODD Protocol for Describing Agent-Based and Other Simulation Models
EvidenceExamplesPositive: The model is documented following ODD with sections for Purpose and patterns, Entities/state variables/scales, Process overview and scheduling, Design concepts, Initialization, Input data, and Submodels.
We provide an ODD-style ABM description including adaptation, stochasticity, sensing and interaction assumptions, plus submodel equations to support reimplementation.
This simulation methods paper reports model rationale, process scheduling, and fitness-for-purpose evaluation criteria alongside full submodel details.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: The study reports odds ratios from logistic regression but contains no simulation model description framework.
A clinical paper discusses unusual or odd symptoms and has no agent-based or simulation model documentation.
The manuscript presents a statistical analysis workflow without entities, scheduling, or submodel definitions.
Matching.Explain: Apply as a reporting overlay when manuscripts describe or should describe simulation-model structure and implementation in ODD-like form, especially agent-based and individual-based models where full-text method sections contain the strongest cues.

Name: PRISMA 2020
LongName: Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses 2020 Statement
Description: PRISMA 2020 provides an updated set of evidence-based reporting items for systematic reviews and meta-analyses. It aims to ensure transparent, complete, and reproducible reporting of the rationale, methods, results, and interpretation of systematic reviews.
Aim: To improve the transparency, completeness, and reproducibility of reporting in systematic reviews and meta-analyses, enabling readers to assess methods, risk of bias, certainty of evidence, and applicability of findings.

Scope.Coverage: Completed systematic reviews with or without meta-analysis evaluating health-related interventions, exposures, diagnostics, prognostics, or other clinical and public health questions.
Scope.Focus: Comprehensive reporting of review rationale, objectives, eligibility criteria, information sources, search strategies, study selection, data collection, risk of bias assessment, synthesis methods, results, certainty of evidence, and funding.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not for primary studies such as randomized trials (use CONSORT), observational studies (use STROBE), or trial protocols (use SPIRIT). Not for scoping reviews unless adapted PRISMA-ScR is explicitly used.

Aliases: Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses
EvidenceExamplesPositive: This systematic review and meta-analysis was conducted in accordance with PRISMA 2020.
We followed PRISMA guidelines and present a PRISMA flow diagram of study selection.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: We conducted a randomized controlled trial and report according to CONSORT.
This observational cohort study is reported according to STROBE.
This clinical practice guideline was developed and reported with AGREE II.
Matching.Explain: Matches primary reports of completed systematic reviews and meta-analyses that explicitly frame review methods or PRISMA reporting; excludes guideline-development manuscripts, protocols, and primary studies.

Name: QMARS
LongName: Qualitative Meta-Analysis Article Reporting Standards
Description: QMARS is APA's reporting standard for qualitative meta-analyses, including metasyntheses, meta-ethnographies, critical interpretive syntheses, and related forms of qualitative secondary analysis.
Aim: To improve transparency and methodological integrity in qualitative meta-analyses by specifying what should be reported about synthesis goals, study selection, researcher reflexivity, study characteristics, analytic procedures, and interpretation of the reviewed literature.

Scope.Coverage: Qualitative meta-analyses in psychology and related social sciences that aggregate or integrate qualitative findings across primary studies.
Scope.Focus: Problem framing, synthesis type, study-selection strategy, researcher positioning, reviewed-study characteristics, secondary analytic procedures, methodological integrity, contextualized findings, and interpretation of the reviewed literature.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not for primary qualitative studies and not for quantitative meta-analyses. QMARS focuses on secondary synthesis of qualitative findings rather than collection of new qualitative data.

EvidenceExamplesPositive: A qualitative metasynthesis reports search and inclusion logic, reviewed-study characteristics, interpretive coding procedures, contextual contradictions across studies, and transferability limits.
A meta-ethnography in psychology describes researcher positioning, iterative selection of studies, and the synthesis of themes across primary reports.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A primary interview study should use JARS-Qual rather than QMARS.
A quantitative meta-analysis with pooled effect sizes belongs with a quantitative review standard rather than QMARS.
Matching.Explain: Use QMARS for qualitative meta-analyses and related qualitative research syntheses that aggregate or integrate findings across primary qualitative studies.

Name: QTD
LongName: Qualitative Transparency Deliberations
Description: QTD is a qualitative political-science transparency overlay distilled from the Qualitative Transparency Deliberations. It emphasizes context-sensitive research explicitness over one-size-fits-all transparency rules, focusing on how evidence was generated, how analysis proceeded, how human participants were protected, and how data-sharing choices should be justified in relation to methodology, ethics, and research context.
Aim: To guide qualitative political-science scholarship toward forms of openness and explicitness that improve interpretability and evaluability while respecting methodological diversity, ethical obligations, and the risks faced by human participants and their communities.

Scope.Coverage: Qualitative political-science and adjacent social-science manuscripts, especially work using interviews, ethnography, textual evidence, process tracing, interpretive methods, or research in sensitive political settings.
Scope.Focus: Context-sensitive transparency about evidence generation, analytic process, positionality, human-subjects risks, and selective or restricted sharing of source materials.
Scope.ContrastWith: QTD is not a maximalist data-sharing checklist and not a universal meta-standard for all research. It complements broader transparency overlays such as DA-RT by foregrounding methodological pluralism and the priority of participant protection in qualitative inquiry.

EvidenceExamplesPositive: A qualitative political-science study based on interviews explains participant risks, field access, source selection, and how evidence was interpreted without fully sharing raw transcripts.
An ethnographic manuscript in a repressive setting justifies limited data sharing, provides a detailed analytic narrative, and reflects on positionality and participant protection.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A purely quantitative article with open data and code but no qualitative evidence-generation or participant-protection issues is better matched to a different transparency overlay.
A non-empirical political-theory essay with no fieldwork, interviews, sources, or empirical claims should not map strongly to QTD.
Matching.Explain: Use QTD for qualitative political-science manuscripts that need context-sensitive transparency about evidence generation, analytic process, source sharing, and ethical protection of human participants.

Name: REFORMS
LongName: Reporting Standards for Machine Learning Based Science
Description: REFORMS is a cross-disciplinary reporting checklist for research manuscripts that use machine learning models as evidence for scientific claims. It organizes 32 checklist items across study goals, computational reproducibility, data quality, preprocessing, modeling, data leakage, metrics and uncertainty, and generalizability.
Aim: To improve the validity, reproducibility, and generalizability of ML-based science by making the scientific claim, modeling workflow, evaluation choices, leakage risks, and limits of applicability explicit and auditable.

Scope.Coverage: Empirical manuscripts that use machine learning model performance as evidence for a scientific claim, including prediction, detection, measurement, or related analytic tasks carried out for scientific rather than purely product or benchmark purposes.
Scope.Focus: Target population and study goals, reproducibility materials, data provenance and suitability, preprocessing, model specification and selection, leakage prevention, metrics and uncertainty reporting, and explicit discussion of external validity and limits.
Scope.ContrastWith: REFORMS is tailored to ML-based science rather than general ML methods papers or non-ML quantitative studies. It complements design-specific guidelines and is additive to field-specific requirements such as preregistration, ethics review, or clinical reporting standards.

Aliases: Reporting Standards for Machine Learning Based Science
EvidenceExamplesPositive: A manuscript uses machine learning model performance to support a scientific claim and needs reporting guidance on study goals, data provenance, modeling decisions, leakage prevention, uncertainty, and generalizability.
An interdisciplinary ML paper in health, social science, or natural science needs a field-agnostic checklist focused on scientific use of ML rather than pure benchmark-style ML methods research.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A pure ML methods paper focused on advancing a benchmark or architecture without making a scientific claim about a real-world population is not the central REFORMS use case.
A non-ML quantitative manuscript using only traditional explanatory modeling or simulation without machine learning would not meaningfully match REFORMS.
Matching.Explain: Use REFORMS for empirical research manuscripts that use machine learning as evidence for scientific claims and need a cross-disciplinary reporting checklist spanning the full ML-based science workflow.

Name: RGSR
LongName: Reporting Guidelines for Simulation-Based Research in Social Sciences
Description: A reporting framework for simulation-based research in the social sciences, introduced with a focus on system dynamics research. The guideline separates minimum and preferred requirements for reporting models, simulation experiments, optimization experiments, and key visualization practices needed to support reproducibility and efficient communication.
Aim: Improve reproducibility, transparency, and efficient communication in simulation-based social science manuscripts by requiring enough information to recreate models, repeat simulation experiments, and reproduce optimization analyses.

Scope.Coverage: Simulation-based research manuscripts in the social sciences, especially those using system dynamics and related computational modeling approaches to generate numerical results.
Scope.Focus: Model structure and equations, base-case reproduction, units, data sources, source code access, simulation platform and algorithm details, scenario specifications, uncertainty and stochastic reporting, optimization objective and search procedures, and visualization choices that reduce the cost of independent reproduction.
Scope.ContrastWith: RGSR is broader than a single modeling-platform checklist and is focused on manuscript reporting for reproducibility. It does not prescribe how a system should be modeled or analyzed, and it is not limited to one simulation paradigm.

EvidenceExamplesPositive: A system dynamics paper reports model equations, parameter values, units, simulation platform, scenario settings, stochastic iterations, and optimization procedures and needs a reproducibility-focused reporting framework.
A computational social science manuscript presents simulation experiments with sensitivity analysis, uncertainty summaries, and platform-specific details needed for independent reproduction.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A purely theoretical social-science manuscript with no computational model, simulation runs, or optimization experiments is not an RGSR manuscript.
An empirical observational study that only uses standard statistical regression without a simulation model or scenario-based computational experiments should not map to RGSR.
Matching.Explain: Use RGSR for social-science manuscripts in which simulation models generate reported results and readers need enough detail to reproduce the model, rerun experiments, and assess optimization procedures.

Name: RoB 2
LongName: Revised Cochrane Risk of Bias Tool for Randomized Trials (Individually Randomized Trials Guidance)
Description: A structured risk-of-bias assessment tool for randomized trials. This guidance document explains how to apply RoB 2.0 to individually randomized trials, including the preliminary setup of the assessment, the five mandatory bias domains, the use of signalling questions, and the logic for reaching domain-level and overall risk-of-bias judgements.
Aim: Assess the risk of bias for a particular result from a randomized trial by using structured signalling questions across mandatory bias domains and then reaching domain-level and overall judgements that are consistent and transparent.
Keywords: bias arising from the randomization process

Scope.Coverage: Assessment of risk of bias for specific results from randomized trials, especially individually randomized and parallel-group designs, with extensions noted for cluster-randomized and cross-over trials.
Scope.Focus: Specifying the target result and effect of interest, answering signalling questions independently, judging five mandatory bias domains, and combining those judgements into an overall risk-of-bias assessment.
Scope.ContrastWith: RoB 2 is a study-appraisal tool, not a manuscript-reporting standard. It is used to judge the trustworthiness of reported trial results rather than to tell authors how to write a trial report.

Aliases: Revised Cochrane risk of bias tool for randomized trials

Patterns: \beffect of starting and adhering to intervention\b

EvidenceExamplesPositive: A systematic review of randomized trials assesses each trial result using RoB 2 domains such as the randomization process, missing outcome data, and selection of the reported result.
A review team needs a structured tool for judging risk of bias for a specific estimate from an individually randomized trial, including whether the target effect is assignment or adherence.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: An observational study or nonrandomized intervention study should not be assessed with RoB 2; a nonrandomized risk-of-bias tool such as ROBINS-I would be more appropriate.
A manuscript-reporting checklist request is not a RoB 2 use case, because RoB 2 appraises study credibility rather than prescribing article structure.
Matching.Explain: Use RoB 2 for structured risk-of-bias assessment of results from randomized trials, especially individually randomized or parallel-group designs, when a review or evidence synthesis needs transparent domain-level and overall bias judgements.

Name: SAMPL
LongName: Statistical Analyses and Methods in the Published Literature (SAMPL)
Description: SAMPL provides practical reporting guidance for statistical methods and statistical results so readers can understand, verify, and interpret quantitative findings clearly.
Aim: To improve clarity, transparency, and completeness in reporting statistical analyses and results in manuscripts that use quantitative methods.

Scope.Coverage: Manuscripts that report quantitative analyses or statistical results, especially where readers need enough detail to understand what was done, what was estimated, and how uncertainty was expressed.
Scope.Focus: Clear reporting of statistical methods, descriptive summaries, effect estimates, precision, exact p-values, multiple-comparison handling, missing data handling, subgroup analyses, and key analysis assumptions.
Scope.ContrastWith: SAMPL complements study-design-specific reporting guidelines such as CONSORT, STROBE, PRISMA, and STARD. It focuses specifically on the statistical reporting layer rather than the full design-specific reporting structure.

Aliases: Statistical Analyses and Methods in the Published Literature
EvidenceExamplesPositive: The manuscript reports effect estimates, confidence intervals, and exact p-values for the primary and secondary analyses.
We used multivariable logistic regression and report adjusted odds ratios with 95% confidence intervals.
Between-group comparisons were assessed with t-tests and repeated-measures ANOVA, with p-values reported for the main outcomes.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: The paper is qualitative and does not report any quantitative statistical analyses.
This manuscript is a narrative commentary without statistical methods or statistical results.
Matching.Explain: Adds a supporting statistical-reporting guide when the manuscript clearly contains quantitative statistical analyses.

Name: SANRA
LongName: Scale for the Assessment of Narrative Review Articles
Description: A six-item appraisal scale for evaluating the quality of narrative review articles in medicine. SANRA is designed for editors, reviewers, readers, and authors assessing non-systematic review manuscripts, with each item scored from 0 to 2 and supported by anchor definitions and usage instructions.
Aim: Assess and improve the quality of narrative review manuscripts by checking whether the review justifies its importance, states clear aims, describes its literature search, supports key claims with references, reasons scientifically, and presents relevant endpoint data appropriately.

Scope.Coverage: Medical and health-science narrative review manuscripts that summarize a topic without using a fully systematic-review design.
Scope.Focus: Justification of the review's importance, statement of aims or questions, transparency about the literature search, completeness of referencing, quality of scientific reasoning, and appropriate presentation of relevant endpoint data.
Scope.ContrastWith: SANRA is a short critical appraisal tool rather than a full reporting guideline. It is intended for non-systematic narrative reviews and complements, rather than replaces, more formal reporting standards for systematic reviews or primary studies.

Aliases: Scale for the Assessment of Narrative Review Articles
EvidenceExamplesPositive: A medical narrative review manuscript explains why the topic matters, states clear review aims, describes how literature was gathered, supports key claims with references, and synthesizes endpoint data in an argument-driven way.
An editor or reviewer is evaluating a non-systematic review article and wants a short checklist focused on narrative-review quality rather than systematic-review reporting.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A systematic review or meta-analysis with protocol registration, formal study selection, and risk-of-bias methods is better matched to systematic-review standards than to SANRA.
A primary clinical trial or observational study manuscript with no review-style synthesis should not map to SANRA.
Matching.Explain: Use SANRA for narrative review manuscripts in medicine and health when the task is to assess or improve the core quality of a non-systematic review article.

Name: SQUIRE
LongName: Revised Standards for Quality Improvement Reporting Excellence (SQUIRE 2.0)
Description: SQUIRE 2.0 provides a framework for reporting new knowledge about how to improve healthcare. It is intended for reports describing system-level work to improve the quality, safety, and value of healthcare, using methods to establish the association between observed outcomes and intervention(s).
Aim: To assist authors in writing clearly, precisely and completely about systematic efforts to improve the quality, safety and value of healthcare services.

Scope.Coverage: Reports of systematic, data-driven efforts to improve healthcare quality, safety, effectiveness, patient-centeredness, timeliness, efficiency, cost, equity, or access, including qualitative and quantitative evaluations of interventions and their impact in real-world healthcare systems.
Scope.Focus: Transparent reporting of the local problem, rationale (including theory), context, intervention details, study methods used to assess impact and attribution, measures and analysis (including variation over time), results (including evolution and unintended consequences), and interpretation, limitations, conclusions, and funding.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not a study-design-specific guideline like CONSORT (randomized trials) or STROBE (observational epidemiology). SQUIRE focuses on healthcare improvement work and the study of improvement interventions, and may be used alongside intervention-description guidance (e.g., TIDieR) when appropriate.

Aliases: Standards for Quality Improvement Reporting Excellence
EvidenceExamplesPositive: We conducted a quality improvement initiative using PDSA cycles to reduce catheter-associated infections and evaluated outcomes over time using statistical process control.
This healthcare improvement project reports context, intervention details, process and outcome measures, and analysis of variation over time.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: We performed a randomized controlled trial and report according to CONSORT.
This protocol describes a planned trial and will be reported using SPIRIT.
Matching.Explain: Maps healthcare improvement / quality improvement reports (including evaluations of improvement interventions) to SQUIRE 2.0, while excluding randomized trial reporting, observational epidemiology guidelines, evidence syntheses, and case reports.

Name: SRQR
LongName: Standards for Reporting Qualitative Research
Description: The Standards for Reporting Qualitative Research (SRQR) provide a 21-item checklist to promote transparency and completeness in reporting qualitative research across disciplines. The guideline specifies essential elements to describe the study’s title, abstract, problem formulation, qualitative approach, context, sampling, data collection, analysis, findings, and considerations such as trustworthiness, limitations, conflicts of interest, and funding.
Aim: To improve the transparency, rigor, and completeness of reporting qualitative research studies so that readers can assess credibility, transferability, and methodological integrity.

Scope.Coverage: Completed qualitative research studies involving human participants, documents, or events, including diverse qualitative methodologies and paradigms.
Scope.Focus: Provides structured reporting standards for qualitative study design, researcher reflexivity, context, sampling, ethics, data collection and processing, analysis, trustworthiness, findings, integration with prior work, limitations, conflicts of interest, and funding.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not intended for randomized trials, observational quantitative studies, systematic reviews, or case reports. Unlike CONSORT or STROBE, SRQR addresses qualitative methodologies and interpretive paradigms rather than quantitative effect estimation.

EvidenceExamplesPositive: We conducted a qualitative grounded theory study using semi-structured interviews.
This ethnographic study explores clinicians’ experiences through thematic analysis.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: We conducted a randomized controlled trial.
This systematic review and meta-analysis followed PRISMA guidelines.
Matching.Explain: Maps qualitative research reports to SRQR, excluding quantitative trials, observational studies, and evidence syntheses.

Name: STARD
LongName: STARD 2015: An Updated List of Essential Items for Reporting Diagnostic Accuracy Studies
Description: STARD 2015 is a reporting guideline that specifies essential items for complete and transparent reporting of diagnostic accuracy studies. It aims to improve the quality of reporting to enable readers to assess risk of bias, applicability, and validity of diagnostic test evaluations.
Aim: To enhance the completeness and transparency of reporting in studies of diagnostic accuracy, enabling critical appraisal of bias, applicability, and the validity of estimates of test performance.

Scope.Coverage: Primary studies evaluating the accuracy of one or more index tests against a reference standard in human participants.
Scope.Focus: Transparent reporting of participant selection, index test and reference standard conduct, statistical methods, and accuracy estimates to allow assessment of bias and applicability.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not intended for randomized trials of diagnostic strategies, prognostic model studies, or purely analytical validation studies without clinical accuracy evaluation.

Aliases: Standards for Reporting Diagnostic Accuracy Studies
Matching.Explain: Matches primary diagnostic accuracy studies evaluating an index test against a reference standard.

Name: Statistics in Urology
LongName: Guidelines for Reporting of Statistics for Clinical Research in Urology
Description: A cross-cutting statistical reporting guideline for clinical research manuscripts in urology. It addresses common problems in study-design reporting, hypothesis testing, p-value interpretation, estimation, time-to-event analysis, multivariable modeling, diagnostic-test reporting, and the wording of conclusions.
Aim: To improve the statistical analysis, reporting, and interpretation of clinical research articles by identifying common errors and specifying practical recommendations for design reporting, estimation, inference, modeling, and interpretation.

Scope.Coverage: Clinical research manuscripts, especially in urology, that report statistical analyses of interventions, outcomes, predictors, diagnostic tests, prognostic models, or evidence synthesis.
Scope.Focus: Transparent reporting of cohort selection and randomization, appropriate description of statistical methods, correct interpretation of p-values and confidence intervals, emphasis on effect estimates, careful handling of time-to-event analyses, sound multivariable and diagnostic modeling, and clinically meaningful conclusions.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not a disease-specific treatment guideline and not a substitute for design-specific reporting statements such as CONSORT, STROBE, TRIPOD, or AMSTAR. It is a cross-cutting statistical overlay for clinical research reporting.

Aliases: Guidelines for Reporting of Statistics for Clinical Research in Urology
EvidenceExamplesPositive: A clinical paper discusses p-values, confidence intervals, Kaplan-Meier curves, multivariable models, or diagnostic-test performance and needs a statistical reporting overlay.
A urology outcomes manuscript reports time-to-event analyses, hazard ratios, calibration, or decision curves and would benefit from this guideline.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A purely qualitative study without statistical inference is not a match.
A lab protocol or disease-management guideline is not the target of this reporting standard.
Matching.Explain: Use this guideline as a statistical reporting overlay for clinical research manuscripts, especially in urology, when the manuscript needs help with design reporting, inference, estimation, time-to-event methods, multivariable modeling, diagnostic-test reporting, and interpretation.

Name: Statistics in Urology: Design
LongName: Guidelines for Reporting of Statistics for Clinical Research in Urology: Design
Description: Design-focused statistical reporting overlay for clinical research manuscripts in urology. It covers study-design reporting, cohort selection, randomization, and sufficiently detailed statistical methods.
Aim: Improve reporting of study design and statistical analysis setup in urology manuscripts by making cohort selection, randomization, study questions, and analysis methods transparent and reproducible.

Scope.Coverage: Clinical research manuscripts, especially in urology, that report statistical analyses of interventions, outcomes, predictors, diagnostic tests, prognostic models, or evidence synthesis.

LongName: Guidelines for Reporting of Statistics for Clinical Research in Urology: Estimates
Description: Estimation-focused statistical reporting overlay for clinical research manuscripts in urology. It emphasizes effect sizes, confidence intervals, time-to-event reporting, and clear presentation of study estimates.
Aim: Improve reporting of estimates in urology manuscripts by emphasizing effect sizes, confidence intervals, precision, clinically interpretable comparisons, and careful reporting of time-to-event analyses.

Name: Statistics in Urology: Inference
LongName: Guidelines for Reporting of Statistics for Clinical Research in Urology: Inference and P-Values
Description: Inference-focused statistical reporting overlay for clinical research manuscripts in urology. It addresses p-value interpretation, null-hypothesis language, multiplicity, and other common inferential mistakes.
Aim: Improve interpretation of hypothesis tests and p-values in urology manuscripts by discouraging misleading null-hypothesis language, overclaiming from near-threshold p-values, and poorly handled multiplicity.

Scope.Coverage: Clinical research manuscripts, especially in urology, that report statistical analyses of interventions, outcomes, predictors, diagnostic tests, prognostic models, or evidence synthesis.
Scope.Focus: Transparent reporting of cohort selection and randomization, appropriate description of statistical methods, correct interpretation of p-values and confidence intervals, emphasis on effect estimates, careful handling of time-to-event analyses, sound multivariable and diagnostic modeling, and clinically meaningful conclusions.

LongName: Guidelines for Reporting of Statistics for Clinical Research in Urology: Interpretation
Description: Interpretation-focused statistical reporting overlay for clinical research manuscripts in urology. It focuses on clinically meaningful conclusions, limitations, and avoiding exaggerated causal or practical claims.
Aim: Improve interpretation in urology manuscripts by encouraging conclusions that reflect the actual results, acknowledge uncertainty and limitations, and avoid overstated causal or clinical claims.

LongName: Guidelines for Reporting of Statistics for Clinical Research in Urology: Multivariable Models and Diagnostic Tests

Scope.Coverage: Clinical research manuscripts, especially in urology, that report statistical analyses of interventions, outcomes, predictors, diagnostic tests, prognostic models, or evidence synthesis.
Scope.Focus: Transparent reporting of cohort selection and randomization, appropriate description of statistical methods, correct interpretation of p-values and confidence intervals, emphasis on effect estimates, careful handling of time-to-event analyses, sound multivariable and diagnostic modeling, and clinically meaningful conclusions.

Name: STREAMS
LongName: STREAMS guidelines: standards for technical reporting in environmental and host-associated microbiome studies
Description: STREAMS provides technical reporting standards for environmental, synthetic, and non-human host-associated microbiome studies, extending STORMS toward broader microbiome domains and machine-actionable reporting.
Aim: To improve completeness, standardization, and FAIR-aligned technical reporting in environmental and host-associated microbiome research, including metadata, multi-omics methods, controls, analyses, and data/code access.

Scope.Coverage: Microbiome manuscripts involving environmental, synthetic, or non-human host-associated systems with sequencing and/or other omics data generation, analysis, and reporting requirements across metadata, methods, and access sections.
Scope.Focus: Structured technical reporting of study design, sample/environment context, collection and processing methods, controls, omics generation metadata, bioinformatics/statistical analysis, bias and sensitivity assessment, and data/code/metadata accessibility.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not a primary study-design guideline for randomized trials, observational epidemiology, or systematic reviews. STREAMS is a microbiome technical-reporting overlay and is not intended for non-microbiome papers that merely mention streams (for example hydrology, media streaming, or network traffic).

Aliases: standards for technical reporting in environmental and host-associated microbiome studies
Strengthening the Organization and Reporting of Microbiome Studies
EvidenceExamplesPositive: We profiled soil microbiomes using shotgun metagenomics and report MIxS-compliant metadata, sample collection context, controls, bioinformatics workflow versions, and repository accession identifiers.
This host-associated microbiome study details sample storage, extraction methods, 16S amplification primers, contamination controls, normalization procedures, and code/data availability statements.

Our synthetic community microbiome manuscript reports positive and negative controls, sequencing platform settings, database versions for taxonomy assignment, sensitivity analyses, and FAIR-aligned metadata deposition.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: This hydrology paper analyzes streamflow forecasting and watershed discharge without microbiome sequencing, metadata standards, or omics workflows.
The manuscript describes a video streaming optimization system and does not involve environmental or host-associated microbiome data generation.
This ecological field report mentions streams and water quality but includes no microbiome profiling, sequencing methods, or microbiome data repositories.
Matching.Explain: Apply as a microbiome discipline overlay for manuscripts generating or analyzing environmental, synthetic, or non-human host-associated microbiome omics data, especially when methods/metadata/repository reporting detail appears in full text even without explicit STREAMS citation.

Name: STRESS
LongName: Strengthening the Reporting of Empirical Simulation Studies (STRESS) guidelines
Description: STRESS provides structured reporting checklists for empirical simulation studies to improve clarity, reproducibility, and reuse across discrete-event simulation, agent-based simulation, and system dynamics.
Aim: To strengthen transparent and reproducible reporting of empirical simulation studies by ensuring objectives, model logic, data, experimentation, implementation, and model-sharing details are completely documented.

Scope.Coverage: Empirical simulation manuscripts that build, calibrate, verify, validate, and experiment with DES, ABS, SD, Monte Carlo, queueing, or hybrid simulation models in applied settings.
Scope.Focus: Complete and auditable reporting of simulation objectives, model structure and logic, parameter/data provenance, preprocessing assumptions, scenario design, run configuration (initialisation, warm-up, run length, replications), random sampling controls, implementation environment, and model/code access for reuse.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not applicable to non-simulation uses of the term stress (for example psychological stress, PTSD, stress testing in cardiology, or material/structural stress analyses) unless the manuscript is explicitly an empirical simulation-study report.

Aliases: Strengthening the Reporting of Empirical Simulation Studies
EvidenceExamplesPositive: We developed a discrete-event simulation of emergency department flow and report model objectives, entity logic, warm-up period, run length, number of replications, and random seed handling.
An agent-based model study evaluates intervention scenarios with explicit base-model logic, scenario logic changes, calibration, verification and validation, and a model-sharing statement.
This system dynamics simulation reports stock-flow structure, parameter sources, preprocessing assumptions, simulation experiment setup, and implementation details needed for replication.
The manuscript compares what-if scenarios in a queueing simulation and provides input parameter distributions, random number generator details, and confidence-interval estimation across replications.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: This manuscript studies psychological stress and depression scores in students and does not describe any empirical simulation model.
The paper reports post-traumatic stress disorder prevalence and logistic regression analysis, with no DES, ABS, SD, or simulation experimentation.
This article evaluates cardiac exercise stress tests in patients and includes no simulation model logic, run configuration, or replication setup.
The study investigates tensile and stress-strain properties of composite materials and is not an empirical simulation-study reporting manuscript.
Matching.Explain: Use as a reporting-overlay support guide for empirical simulation manuscripts when DES/ABS/SD or equivalent simulation-experiment language appears in methods/implementation text, even if the paper does not explicitly cite STRESS.

Name: STROBE
LongName: The Strengthening the Reporting of Observational Studies in Epidemiology (STROBE) Statement: guidelines for reporting observational studies
Description: The STROBE Statement provides a checklist of essential items that should be addressed in reports of observational studies, including cohort, case-control, and cross-sectional studies. Its purpose is to improve transparency, reproducibility, and completeness in reporting epidemiological research by specifying what information authors should disclose in each section of an article.
Aim: To improve the reporting quality of observational research by ensuring that authors clearly describe study design, methods, results, and interpretation for cohort, case-control, and cross-sectional studies.

Scope.Coverage: This guideline applies to reports of observational studies in epidemiology, specifically cohort studies, case-control studies, and cross-sectional studies. It covers primary research articles reporting original observational data in health-related research. It does not apply to randomized controlled trials, systematic reviews, meta-analyses, case reports, qualitative research, or purely methodological/statistical simulation papers without primary observational data.
Scope.Focus: Observational study reporting quality: clear design description, participant flow and selection, exposure/outcome definitions, confounding and bias handling, statistical methods, risk estimates, missing data, and sensitivity analyses.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not for randomized controlled trials (CONSORT), systematic reviews/meta-analyses (PRISMA/MOOSE/ENTREQ), case reports (CARE), qualitative primary studies (SRQR), diagnostic accuracy studies (STARD), or prediction model studies (TRIPOD).

Aliases: STROBE Statement: Strengthening the Reporting of Observational Studies in Epidemiology
Matching.Explain: General observational studies should map to STROBE.

Name: TEN-SPIDERS
LongName: TEN-SPIDERS reporting tool for the proportion of days covered method
Description: TEN-SPIDERS is a reporting tool for studies that calculate medication adherence using the proportion of days covered (PDC) method. It focuses on the PDC-specific parameters that often vary or go unreported, including threshold choice, sample eligibility, numerator and denominator definitions, survival handling, presupply, in-hospital supplies, dosing information, early refills, and switching.
Aim: To improve the transparent and systematic reporting of the proportion of days covered method so that medication-adherence estimates can be interpreted reliably and compared across studies using equivalent assumptions.

Scope.Coverage: Studies that assess medication adherence using the proportion of days covered method, especially those based on administrative or dispensing data for long-term medicines.
Scope.Focus: Transparent reporting of PDC-specific design choices and assumptions, including threshold definition, eligibility criteria, numerator and denominator construction, handling of death, presupply, in-hospital supplies, dose imputation, stockpiling, and therapeutic switching.
Scope.ContrastWith: TEN-SPIDERS is a method-specific reporting tool for the PDC adherence measure. It complements broader reporting frameworks such as EMERGE and RECORD-PE rather than replacing them.

Aliases: proportion of days covered reporting tool
EvidenceExamplesPositive: A pharmacoepidemiology study measures adherence using proportion of days covered and explains how stockpiling, therapeutic switching, hospital stays, and dose imputation were handled.

An administrative-data study of long-term cardiovascular medicines defines the PDC numerator and denominator, justifies its adherence threshold, and reports whether the cohort was limited to new users.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A medication-adherence paper using only self-report scales without any proportion-of-days-covered calculation is not a TEN-SPIDERS manuscript.
A general pharmacoepidemiology study that mentions adherence conceptually but does not derive or report PDC-specific parameters should not map strongly to TEN-SPIDERS.
Matching.Explain: Use TEN-SPIDERS for studies that calculate medication adherence with the proportion of days covered method and need transparent reporting of the PDC-specific parameters that drive those estimates.

Name: TIDieR
LongName: Template for Intervention Description and Replication (TIDieR) checklist and guide
Description: TIDieR is a 12-item intervention-reporting checklist and guide designed to improve completeness of intervention descriptions so studies can be interpreted, replicated, and implemented.
Aim: To ensure interventions are described in enough detail for replication and practical implementation, including rationale, materials, procedures, provider details, delivery mode, dose, tailoring, modifications, and fidelity.
Keywords: Template for Intervention Description and Replication

Scope.Coverage: Any manuscript that evaluates or documents one or more interventions and needs a reproducible, implementation-ready intervention description, including trials, protocols, and other intervention-focused designs.
Scope.Focus: Intervention rationale, materials, procedures, provider qualifications, delivery mode, setting, schedule/dose, personalization, protocol modifications, and planned/actual fidelity or adherence.
Scope.ContrastWith: TIDieR is not a full design-level reporting standard by itself; it is an intervention-description overlay intended to be used alongside primary design guidelines such as CONSORT or SPIRIT and other study-design checklists.

Aliases: Template for Intervention Description and Replication
EvidenceExamplesPositive: The intervention is described using TIDieR including materials, procedures, provider training, delivery mode, and dose schedule.
We reported intervention fidelity with planned and actual adherence and described all protocol modifications.
This nonpharmacologic trial uses CONSORT with TIDieR as an extension for intervention details.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: The paper mentions tidy data and tidyverse packages but does not report an intervention study.
This manuscript is a molecular assay protocol with no intervention providers, delivery mode, or fidelity reporting context.
The article is a narrative editorial without intervention procedures or dose/schedule reporting.
Matching.Explain: Adds an intervention-description reporting overlay when manuscripts evaluate interventions and need reproducible details on materials, procedures, delivery, dosing, tailoring, and fidelity.

Name: TRIPOD+AI
LongName: TRIPOD+AI statement: updated guidance for reporting clinical prediction models that use regression or machine learning methods
Description: TRIPOD/TRIPOD+AI provides reporting guidance for studies that develop prediction models, scores, classifiers, biomarker signatures, or evaluate model performance in healthcare, regardless of whether regression or machine learning methods are used.
Aim: To improve complete, accurate, and transparent reporting of prediction model development and evaluation studies in healthcare, including studies using machine learning methods.

Scope.Coverage: Healthcare studies that develop a multivariable prediction model or evaluate its performance, including studies using regression and machine learning approaches.
Scope.Focus: Transparent reporting of context, data, participant flow, predictors, outcomes, analysis methods, model specification, model performance, fairness considerations, open science practices, and usability in care pathways.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not for randomized trial intervention reporting (CONSORT), observational association-only reporting (STROBE), diagnostic test accuracy reporting without a multivariable prediction-model/signature focus (STARD), or systematic review reporting (PRISMA). If the manuscript explicitly states that it was performed or reported according to TRIPOD and develops or validates a diagnostic/prognostic model, score, signature, classifier, or risk tool, TRIPOD+AI remains applicable alongside STARD when both guide types fit.

Aliases: Transparent Reporting of a multivariable prediction model for Individual Prognosis Or Diagnosis
EvidenceExamplesPositive: We developed and externally validated a prognostic prediction model for 30-day mortality and report calibration slope, c-statistic, and decision-curve analysis.
This study evaluates a machine-learning diagnostic prediction model in a multicentre cohort and reports discrimination and calibration with confidence intervals.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: This randomized trial compares two treatments and does not develop or evaluate a multivariable prediction model.
This paper is a systematic review of prediction studies and does not report primary model development or model performance evaluation.
Matching.Explain: This catalog item covers TRIPOD and TRIPOD+AI prediction-model reporting. Select it for healthcare manuscripts that develop or evaluate diagnostic/prognostic prediction models, scores, classifiers, or biomarker signatures. It can apply alongside STARD when a diagnostic-accuracy manuscript also develops or validates a multivariable prediction/signature model, especially when the manuscript explicitly says it was reported in accordance with TRIPOD.

Name: TSBS
LongName: Transparency in Social and Behavioral Sciences
Description: A consensus-based transparency checklist for social and behavioural research, especially confirmatory work on primary data. The article introduces the framework and its four sections, while the companion online application provides the full 36-item long checklist used to document preregistration, methods, results and discussion, and data, code, and materials availability.
Aim: Improve and document the transparency of social and behavioural research reports by making key choices about preregistration, methods, analysis, and sharing of research artifacts visible to editors, reviewers, and readers.

Scope.Coverage: Broad social and behavioural research reports, especially confirmatory studies using primary data, where authors need to disclose what was planned, what was done, what changed, and what research artifacts are available.
Scope.Focus: Preregistration timing and content, participant handling and study procedures, transparency about analytical decisions and assumption handling, explicit separation of confirmatory versus exploratory work, and public availability of data, code, and materials.
Scope.ContrastWith: This checklist is a broad transparency overlay rather than a design-specific reporting guideline like CONSORT or PRISMA. It is meant to complement those standards by documenting research-process transparency across many social and behavioural study types.

EvidenceExamplesPositive: A psychology or political-science manuscript distinguishes confirmatory from exploratory analyses, links a preregistration, discloses preprocessing and missing-data decisions, and includes data and code availability information.

A social or behavioural study wants a broad transparency overlay documenting preregistration content, methods decisions, and the availability of research artifacts in addition to any design-specific standard.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A purely theoretical manuscript with no primary data, no empirical methods section, and no research artifacts to share is not a meaningful match for this checklist.
A paper that only needs a narrow study-design checklist and has no broader transparency or availability reporting component is unlikely to need the full Transparency Checklist overlay.
Matching.Explain: Use the Transparency Checklist as a broad social-science transparency overlay for manuscripts that need explicit disclosure of preregistration, methodological choices, confirmatory versus exploratory status, and the availability of data, code, and materials.
